title: Epilepsy — Chronic Management
oneLiner: Long-term antiseizure therapy to prevent recurrence in patients with ≥2 unprovoked seizures; aims for seizure freedom with minimal side effects.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **SUDEP (Sudden Unexpected Death in Epilepsy):** Risk highest with nocturnal generalised tonic-clonic seizures (GTCS), poor adherence, alcohol misuse, polytherapy. *Key finding:* unwitnessed seizure, post-ictal respiratory depression. Prevention = device (Emfit/Seizure-alert), bedroom supervision, optimise therapy.
- **Status epilepticus:** Prolonged seizure >5 min or recurrent without recovery. *Key finding:* ongoing convulsive activity or non-convulsive (GCS <10, subtle twitching) — call for emergency treatment immediately.
- **Suicide risk:** Especially with levetiracetam, perampanel, or topiramate in patients with psychiatric history. *Key finding:* new or worsening depression, withdrawal, or expressed ideation — stop drug, refer psychiatry.
sections.Presentation:
- **Seizure recurrence** — breakthrough GTCS, focal aware, or focal impaired awareness
- **Subtherapeutic drug levels** — nausea, dizziness, ataxia (especially phenytoin/carbamazepine)
- **Side effects** — drowsiness, rash, tremor, weight gain, bone marrow suppression
- **Non‑adherence** — missed doses, cost, forgetfulness, pregnancy fear
- **Drug interactions** — new antibiotic (carbapenems reduce valproate), oral contraceptive failure (enzyme-inducing AEDs)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Non‑epileptic attack disorder (NEAD/PNES)** — no epileptic features (asynchronous movements, eyes closed, pelvic thrusting, post-ictal normal), normal interictal EEG. **Splitter:** Video EEG in long‑term monitoring; or suggestible attack with suggestion in clinic.
- **Syncope** — brief loss of consciousness with recovery on lying down, provoked by standing/cough/voiding, no post‑ictal confusion.
- **Transient ischaemic attack / migraine** — focal neurological symptoms without altered consciousness; migraine often has positive visual symptoms (scintillations), TIA negative (field defect).
- **Parasomnias** — at night, complex behaviours with partial arousal on EEG, not stereotyped as epilepsy.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Observe seizure semiology (video if possible); check drug levels if on phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate (trough).
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, calcium, magnesium, glucose; AED levels (phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate, lamotrigine); pregnancy test in women of childbearing age.
- **Imaging:** MRI brain (epilepsy protocol — thin‑section T2/FLAIR, 3D T1) for structural lesion. CT head only if acute presentation or contraindication.
- **Special tests:** Routine outpatient EEG (sleep-deprived) — may show epileptiform discharges; prolonged or ambulatory EEG for diagnostic doubt.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis based on ≥2 unprovoked seizures >24h apart (ILAE). NICE guideline: refer to specialist after first unprovoked seizure if recurrence risk high.
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** in any woman of childbearing age before starting or changing AED — teratogenicity, especially valproate.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** If acute seizure, treat per status protocol (lorazepam/ midazolam). For chronic management, ensure safety — no driving, no swimming alone.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Review adherence, timing of misses.
  - Check drug levels if available (trough).
  - Adjust AED dose (increase by 25–50% if subtherapeutic and no side effects).
  - Switch to alternative if intolerable side effects (e.g., levetiracetam irritability → lamotrigine).
  - Liaise with neurology if breakthrough on high‑dose monotherapy.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Aim for monotherapy first-line.
  - Seizure‑type specific choice (see Drugs & Doses).
  - Titrate slowly (e.g., lamotrigine over 6–8 weeks to avoid rash).
  - Annual review: FBC, LFT, U&E; AED levels only if clinical concern.
  - Women: counsel on contraception, pregnancy, folic acid 5mg daily if planning.
  - Lifestyle: avoid alcohol withdrawal, sleep deprivation, photosensitivity if relevant.
  - Surgical referral if drug‑resistant (failed 2 appropriate AEDs).
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reason):** Continue usual AEDs; check levels; avoid NBM unless urgent procedure; ensure early per‑oral dosing. If NBM, contact neurology for IV equivalent (e.g., levetiracetam IV, phenytoin IV slowly).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (by seizure type):**
  - **Focal:** Lamotrigine (start 25mg OD, titrate by 25–50mg every 2 weeks to target 100–200mg BD), Levetiracetam (start 250–500mg BD, up to 1.5g BD [check local policy]).
  - **Generalised tonic‑clonic:** Valproate (start 200mg BD, titrate to 400–600mg BD — **NOT in women of childbearing potential** unless epilepsy specialist approved, per MHRA) or Levetiracetam (as above).
  - **Absence:** Valproate (as above) or Ethosuximide (start 250mg BD, up to 1g BD [check local policy]).
  - **Myoclonic:** Valproate or Levetiracetam.
- **Common alternatives:** Carbamazepine (focal), Topiramate, Zonisamide, Phenytoin (third‑line due to interactions and side effects).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Valproate: contraindicated in pregnancy (≥10% risk of neural tube defects, lower IQ) — use only under specialist.
  - Enzyme‑inducing AEDs (carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital): reduce COC effectiveness, warfarin, corticosteroids.
  - Lamotrigine: oral contraceptive reduces its level; double dose if on COC.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Levetiracetam: reduce dose if CrCl <50mL/min (e.g., half dose). Valproate: caution in hepatic impairment (check LFTs). Lamotrigine: no major renal dose change but use with caution.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Lamotrigine rapid titration** → Stevens‑Johnson syndrome (SJS) — stick to slow titration schedule. **Valproate in pregnancy** → teratogenicity — always check pregnancy test and avoid. **Phenytoin bolus** → hypotension, bradycardia, or purple glove syndrome — give slowly, not >50mg/min.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Status epilepticus (from missed doses, fever, alcohol), drug rash (SJS with lamotrigine, carbamazepine), hyponatremia (carbamazepine), pancreatitis (valproate), liver failure (valproate).
- **Chronic:** SUDEP, cognitive dulling (topiramate, phenytoin), osteoporosis (enzyme‑inducing AEDs), weight gain (valproate, carbamazepine), gingival hyperplasia (phenytoin), foetal valproate syndrome.
sections.Prognosis: - ~70% become seizure‑free on first appropriate AED. Drug‑resistant epilepsy (failed ≥2 appropriate drugs) has lower chance of remission; may require surgery (temporal lobectomy ≈60–70% seizure‑free). Risk of SUDEP ≈1/1,000 patient‑years (higher with GTCS, non‑adherence, nocturnal seizures).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always ask about contraception and pregnancy plans** in every woman of childbearing age on any AED. Start folic acid 5mg if considering pregnancy.
- **Check AED levels** when seizures recur on stable dose, especially with new drug interactions (e.g., antibiotic course). Trough levels are most useful.
- **Use slow‑release formulations** for carbamazepine and valproate to reduce peak‑related side effects and improve adherence.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Starting lamotrigine without slow titration** — risk of SJS. Must not start in patients on valproate without reducing valproate dose (valproate inhibits lamotrigine clearance, requires slower titration).
- **Stopping an AED abruptly** — can precipitate rebound seizures or status epilepticus. Always taper over 4–8 weeks if withdrawing.
- **Ignoring life‑style factors** — alcohol binge, sleep deprivation, missed doses are the most common triggers. Document in every review.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Seizure duration >5 minutes, >1 seizure in 30 min without full recovery, new focal neurological signs, rash after starting AED, confusion or drowsiness out of proportion.
- **Follow-up:** Outpatient neurology clinic within 6 weeks for dose adjustment, or sooner if breakthrough. Driving DVLA restrictions — must inform DVLA if seizures recurred (revoke licence until seizure‑free for 1 year or as per DVLA rules). Provide written information.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/neurology on‑call for:** Breakthrough GTCS on two appropriate AEDs, signs of status epilepticus (prolonged seizure or repeated without recovery), new severe drug rash (suspected SJS), severely low GCS (NEWS2 ≥6, lactate >4), or suicidal ideation.
- **Call crash team for:** Cardiorespiratory arrest, ongoing convulsive status not responding to benzodiazepine second‑line therapy.

rawMarkdown:
## Epilepsy — Chronic Management
**One-liner:** Long-term antiseizure therapy to prevent recurrence in patients with ≥2 unprovoked seizures; aims for seizure freedom with minimal side effects.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **SUDEP (Sudden Unexpected Death in Epilepsy):** Risk highest with nocturnal generalised tonic-clonic seizures (GTCS), poor adherence, alcohol misuse, polytherapy. *Key finding:* unwitnessed seizure, post-ictal respiratory depression. Prevention = device (Emfit/Seizure-alert), bedroom supervision, optimise therapy.
- **Status epilepticus:** Prolonged seizure >5 min or recurrent without recovery. *Key finding:* ongoing convulsive activity or non-convulsive (GCS <10, subtle twitching) — call for emergency treatment immediately.
- **Suicide risk:** Especially with levetiracetam, perampanel, or topiramate in patients with psychiatric history. *Key finding:* new or worsening depression, withdrawal, or expressed ideation — stop drug, refer psychiatry.

### Presentation
- **Seizure recurrence** — breakthrough GTCS, focal aware, or focal impaired awareness
- **Subtherapeutic drug levels** — nausea, dizziness, ataxia (especially phenytoin/carbamazepine)
- **Side effects** — drowsiness, rash, tremor, weight gain, bone marrow suppression
- **Non‑adherence** — missed doses, cost, forgetfulness, pregnancy fear
- **Drug interactions** — new antibiotic (carbapenems reduce valproate), oral contraceptive failure (enzyme-inducing AEDs)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Non‑epileptic attack disorder (NEAD/PNES)** — no epileptic features (asynchronous movements, eyes closed, pelvic thrusting, post-ictal normal), normal interictal EEG. **Splitter:** Video EEG in long‑term monitoring; or suggestible attack with suggestion in clinic.
- **Syncope** — brief loss of consciousness with recovery on lying down, provoked by standing/cough/voiding, no post‑ictal confusion.
- **Transient ischaemic attack / migraine** — focal neurological symptoms without altered consciousness; migraine often has positive visual symptoms (scintillations), TIA negative (field defect).
- **Parasomnias** — at night, complex behaviours with partial arousal on EEG, not stereotyped as epilepsy.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Observe seizure semiology (video if possible); check drug levels if on phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate (trough).
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, calcium, magnesium, glucose; AED levels (phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate, lamotrigine); pregnancy test in women of childbearing age.
- **Imaging:** MRI brain (epilepsy protocol — thin‑section T2/FLAIR, 3D T1) for structural lesion. CT head only if acute presentation or contraindication.
- **Special tests:** Routine outpatient EEG (sleep-deprived) — may show epileptiform discharges; prolonged or ambulatory EEG for diagnostic doubt.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis based on ≥2 unprovoked seizures >24h apart (ILAE). NICE guideline: refer to specialist after first unprovoked seizure if recurrence risk high.
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** in any woman of childbearing age before starting or changing AED — teratogenicity, especially valproate.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** If acute seizure, treat per status protocol (lorazepam/ midazolam). For chronic management, ensure safety — no driving, no swimming alone.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Review adherence, timing of misses.
  - Check drug levels if available (trough).
  - Adjust AED dose (increase by 25–50% if subtherapeutic and no side effects).
  - Switch to alternative if intolerable side effects (e.g., levetiracetam irritability → lamotrigine).
  - Liaise with neurology if breakthrough on high‑dose monotherapy.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Aim for monotherapy first-line.
  - Seizure‑type specific choice (see Drugs & Doses).
  - Titrate slowly (e.g., lamotrigine over 6–8 weeks to avoid rash).
  - Annual review: FBC, LFT, U&E; AED levels only if clinical concern.
  - Women: counsel on contraception, pregnancy, folic acid 5mg daily if planning.
  - Lifestyle: avoid alcohol withdrawal, sleep deprivation, photosensitivity if relevant.
  - Surgical referral if drug‑resistant (failed 2 appropriate AEDs).
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reason):** Continue usual AEDs; check levels; avoid NBM unless urgent procedure; ensure early per‑oral dosing. If NBM, contact neurology for IV equivalent (e.g., levetiracetam IV, phenytoin IV slowly).

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (by seizure type):**
  - **Focal:** Lamotrigine (start 25mg OD, titrate by 25–50mg every 2 weeks to target 100–200mg BD), Levetiracetam (start 250–500mg BD, up to 1.5g BD [check local policy]).
  - **Generalised tonic‑clonic:** Valproate (start 200mg BD, titrate to 400–600mg BD — **NOT in women of childbearing potential** unless epilepsy specialist approved, per MHRA) or Levetiracetam (as above).
  - **Absence:** Valproate (as above) or Ethosuximide (start 250mg BD, up to 1g BD [check local policy]).
  - **Myoclonic:** Valproate or Levetiracetam.
- **Common alternatives:** Carbamazepine (focal), Topiramate, Zonisamide, Phenytoin (third‑line due to interactions and side effects).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Valproate: contraindicated in pregnancy (≥10% risk of neural tube defects, lower IQ) — use only under specialist.
  - Enzyme‑inducing AEDs (carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital): reduce COC effectiveness, warfarin, corticosteroids.
  - Lamotrigine: oral contraceptive reduces its level; double dose if on COC.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Levetiracetam: reduce dose if CrCl <50mL/min (e.g., half dose). Valproate: caution in hepatic impairment (check LFTs). Lamotrigine: no major renal dose change but use with caution.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Lamotrigine rapid titration** → Stevens‑Johnson syndrome (SJS) — stick to slow titration schedule. **Valproate in pregnancy** → teratogenicity — always check pregnancy test and avoid. **Phenytoin bolus** → hypotension, bradycardia, or purple glove syndrome — give slowly, not >50mg/min.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Status epilepticus (from missed doses, fever, alcohol), drug rash (SJS with lamotrigine, carbamazepine), hyponatremia (carbamazepine), pancreatitis (valproate), liver failure (valproate).
- **Chronic:** SUDEP, cognitive dulling (topiramate, phenytoin), osteoporosis (enzyme‑inducing AEDs), weight gain (valproate, carbamazepine), gingival hyperplasia (phenytoin), foetal valproate syndrome.

### Prognosis
- ~70% become seizure‑free on first appropriate AED. Drug‑resistant epilepsy (failed ≥2 appropriate drugs) has lower chance of remission; may require surgery (temporal lobectomy ≈60–70% seizure‑free). Risk of SUDEP ≈1/1,000 patient‑years (higher with GTCS, non‑adherence, nocturnal seizures).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always ask about contraception and pregnancy plans** in every woman of childbearing age on any AED. Start folic acid 5mg if considering pregnancy.
- **Check AED levels** when seizures recur on stable dose, especially with new drug interactions (e.g., antibiotic course). Trough levels are most useful.
- **Use slow‑release formulations** for carbamazepine and valproate to reduce peak‑related side effects and improve adherence.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Starting lamotrigine without slow titration** — risk of SJS. Must not start in patients on valproate without reducing valproate dose (valproate inhibits lamotrigine clearance, requires slower titration).
- **Stopping an AED abruptly** — can precipitate rebound seizures or status epilepticus. Always taper over 4–8 weeks if withdrawing.
- **Ignoring life‑style factors** — alcohol binge, sleep deprivation, missed doses are the most common triggers. Document in every review.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Seizure duration >5 minutes, >1 seizure in 30 min without full recovery, new focal neurological signs, rash after starting AED, confusion or drowsiness out of proportion.
- **Follow-up:** Outpatient neurology clinic within 6 weeks for dose adjustment, or sooner if breakthrough. Driving DVLA restrictions — must inform DVLA if seizures recurred (revoke licence until seizure‑free for 1 year or as per DVLA rules). Provide written information.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/neurology on‑call for:** Breakthrough GTCS on two appropriate AEDs, signs of status epilepticus (prolonged seizure or repeated without recovery), new severe drug rash (suspected SJS), severely low GCS (NEWS2 ≥6, lactate >4), or suicidal ideation.
- **Call crash team for:** Cardiorespiratory arrest, ongoing convulsive status not responding to benzodiazepine second‑line therapy.
